description:
You are a machine-learning engineer who is about to feed data into an optimisation solver (e.g. SciPy’s L-BFGS-B).  
    A small raw measurement file is already present at  

        /home/user/raw_data/measurements.txt

    It contains five whitespace-delimited rows plus a single header line.  
    Your job is to prepare a clean, solver-ready data set, write a minimal JSON configuration file for the solver, and produce a short log summarising what you did.

    1. Create a new directory
           /home/user/opt_solver
       (create it if it does not exist).

    2. Inside that directory create a CSV file named
           /home/user/opt_solver/input_data.csv
       with *exactly* the following format:

           • The very first line must be the literal header
                 feature1,feature2,target
           • Keep ONLY the rows from measurements.txt that contain no string
                 NaN
             in any column.
           • For each remaining row, copy only the numeric values of columns F1, F2, and target
             (do NOT copy the ID column) and write them as comma-separated values that match the header order.
           • No extra spaces are allowed anywhere in the file.
           • A final newline (line feed, \n) is required at the end of the file.

       The automated test will compare the file byte-for-byte, so adhere exactly to this specification.

    3. Create a JSON configuration file
           /home/user/opt_solver/config.json
       with the following key–value pairs and formatting (including spaces and new-lines):

           {
             "solver": "L-BFGS-B",
             "max_iter": 250,
             "tol": 1e-05,
             "verbose": true
           }

       Make sure that:
           • The file starts with “{” on the first line and ends with “}” on its own line.
           • Indentation is exactly two spaces.
           • A trailing newline (\n) follows the closing brace.

    4. Produce a preparation log
           /home/user/opt_solver/prep_summary.log
       containing exactly three lines, each terminated by a newline:

           DATA_ROWS: <number_of_rows_in_input_data.csv_excluding_header>
           CONFIG_OK: true
           READY_FOR_TRAINING: yes

       For the DATA_ROWS field, write the integer count of data rows that ended up in input_data.csv
       (the header is NOT counted).

    5. When everything is complete, you should be able to verify:

           • /home/user/opt_solver/input_data.csv exists with 4 lines total (1 header + 3 data rows)
           • /home/user/opt_solver/config.json exists and matches the required JSON exactly
           • /home/user/opt_solver/prep_summary.log exists and shows:
                 DATA_ROWS: 3
                 CONFIG_OK: true
                 READY_FOR_TRAINING: yes

    Typical commands that might be useful include mkdir, grep, awk, cut, sed, printf, echo, cat, etc.  
    The entire task can be done with about three or four shell commands, but you may use more if you wish.  
    Do not delete or rename the original measurements.txt file.
truth:
The initial file /home/user/raw_data/measurements.txt already exists with the following exact contents including final newline:

ID F1 F2 target
1 3.5 7.2 10.7
2 NaN 6.1 8.2
3 4.1 5.9 10.0
4 3.9 NaN 9.5
5 4.2 6.5 10.7

    After the agent finishes, the test suite will verify the following:

    1. /home/user/opt_solver/input_data.csv must exist and contain exactly (with trailing newline):

feature1,feature2,target
3.5,7.2,10.7
4.1,5.9,10.0
4.2,6.5,10.7

    2. /home/user/opt_solver/config.json must exist and contain exactly (with trailing newline):

{
  "solver": "L-BFGS-B",
  "max_iter": 250,
  "tol": 1e-05,
  "verbose": true
}

    3. /home/user/opt_solver/prep_summary.log must exist and contain exactly (with trailing newline):

DATA_ROWS: 3
CONFIG_OK: true
READY_FOR_TRAINING: yes

    Permissions: the whole /home/user directory tree is writeable by the current user, so the agent can freely create the opt_solver directory and all three files.
